parts.answer: Which word in lines 1–2 suggests the writer’s state of mind? anxiety

What does the writer expect to find when visiting a castle (paragraph 1, ‘It was with « ’)? • moss-clad / stone building / grey exterior • echoing rooms • bird sounds (moaning of owls / rattling of rooks) 1 mark for each point to a maximum of 2. (Note: candidates can make both points in one of the spaces provided) Using your own words, explain what the writer means by the words underlined in each of the following quotations (paragraph 2, ‘Clytha sits on « ’): 1 mark for each explanation, up to a maximum of 3. Note: the definitions contain the essence of an answer. Be careful not to credit a word actually used in the quoted phrase. However, accept that candidates may respond in different ways, e.g. at greater length. ‘Clytha sits on a curious bump of a hill « ’ (line 7) unusual / unexpected / strange / odd ‘ « all we could spot, at first, were some small pointed towers peering coyly from behind a grove of trees.’ (lines 7–8) shyly / timidly / unassumingly ‘ « we had to proceed up the hill, through an iron gate, and along a meandering overgrown pathway.’ (lines 8–9) winding / wandering / curving / rambling October/November Question Answer Marks Explain how the language in each of these quotations helps to create the atmosphere around the castle when they first arrive. In your answer you should refer to the whole quotation, not just the underlined words. Award 1 mark for a partial explanation of each phrase. Award 2 marks for an explanation of each phrase which shows clear appreciation of the effect of the writer’s use of language. Paraphrase of chosen phrase = 0 for explanation. Explanation must also be predominantly in candidate’s own words. Suggested indicative content: (1) The castle seems like a figure on top of hill looking out over the landscape – the hill is unusual / unsettling / unexpected. (2) It seems as if the castle is hiding / the personification of ‘peering coyly’ might suggest it is hiding / spying on visitors / watching. (3) Getting to the castle invo

Using your own words, explain what the writer thinks is unusual about the Welsh landscape as she drives through it (paragraph 3, ‘It is said « ’). there are lots of castles / hills appear suddenly / with castles hanging onto them 1 mark for a partial explanation, 2 for full explanation in words. Needs hills and castles for 2 marks.

Using your own words, explain how the writer suggests Wales has changed (paragraph 5, ‘By the 19th century « ’). • less wealthy now / had economic problems / poverty • coal mining industry has gone • has a lot of unemployed people 1 mark for a partial explanation, 2 for a full explanation in own words. October/November Question Answer Marks

Using your own words, explain what the writer means by: ‘the fortresses may now assist a country they once helped dominate.’ (lines 23–24) The castles that once ruled over Wales can now help the country (by attracting tourism). 1 mark for a partial explanation, 2 marks for a full explanation. (Award 1 mark for responses that don’t refer to castles but that suggest that attracting tourism can help Wales)

Using your own words, explain how the writer’s daughter reacts to the castle (paragraph 7, ‘One of its four « ’). she is overwhelmed with delight / puts all her belongings on the bed and falls asleep 1 mark for a partial explanation, 2 marks for a full explanation in own words. October/November Question Answer Marks Question 2 This question tests reading assessment objectives R1–R3 (10 marks): R1 demonstrate understanding of explicit meanings R2 demonstrate understanding of implicit meanings and attitudes R3 analyse, evaluate and develop facts, ideas and opinions AND writing assessment objectives W1–W4 (5 marks): W1 articulate experience and express what is thought, felt and imagined W2 sequence facts, ideas and opinions W3 use a range of appropriate vocabulary W4 use register appropriate to audience and context Imagine that you are the writer’s son or daughter. After you have left Clytha Castle you write a journal entry describing your visit. Write the words of your journal entry. In the journal entry you should include the following information: • your first impressions of Clytha Castle • what it was like and how it may have been different from your expectations • what you liked most about the visit. Base your journal entry on what you have read in Passage A, but do not copy from it. Be careful to use your own words. Address each of the three bullets. Begin your journal: ‘I have just left the most unusual place ... ’ Write about 200 to 300 words. Up to 10 marks are available for the content of your answer, and up to 5 marks for the quality of your writing. [15] General notes The most successful responses are likely to select relevant details from the point of view of the child. There will be a sensible and convincing attempt to explain expectations and to convey the reasons for wanting to go to a real castle. Less successful responses are likely to lift sections of the original and do little more than repeat points that are already there without going beyond the m

(a) Notes What do you learn about the structure of Uluru and about Dreamtime, according to Passage B? Write your answers using short notes. Write one point per line. You do not need to use your own words. Up to 10 marks are available for the content

parts.text: 3(a) and (b) on this Question Paper. Question 3 Answer the questions in the order set. (a) Notes What do you learn about the structure of Uluru and about Dreamtime, according to Passage B? Write your answers using short notes. Write one point per line. You do not need to use your own words. Up to 10 marks are available for the content
parts.answer: Reading content for Question 3(a) Give 1 mark per point listed below, up to a maximum of 10. Uluruk: 1 made from sandstone 2 (thought to be) largest monolith in the world 3 318 metres high 4 8 km around 5 extends 2.5 km below ground 6 inselberg / remnant of erosion (of mountain range) 7 was once part of the ocean floor 8 believed to be hollow below ground 9 believed to emit light Dreamtime: 10 called Tjukurpa 11 contains energy source (which is the source of Dreamtime) 12 in Dreamtime there were giants roaming around. 13 in Dreamtime the world was featureless 14 giants / heroes carried out various tasks / camping / making fires / digging for water 15 ended when heroes became tired of tasks 16 part of Aboriginal culture / religion 17 governs all aspects of modern Aboriginal (daily) life (or mention of specific details: ceremonies / rituals / storytelling / artwork) Note: Although lifting of words and phrases from the passage is acceptable it is important that in such cases candidates show evidence of understanding by clearly focusing on the key details; over-lengthy lifting which does not identify specific points should not be rewarded. Credit responses which attempt to use own words and convey the essence of the point. (10) October/November Question Answer Marks

Marking criteria for Question 3(b) Table A, Writing (concision, focus, use of own words) Use the following table to give a mark out of 5 for Writing. Band 1 5 The response is well focused on the passage and the question. All points are expressed clearly, concisely and fluently, and in the candidate’s own words (where appropriate) throughout. Band 2 4 Most points are made clearly and concisely. Own words (where appropriate) are used consistently. The summary is mostly focused but may have a redundant introduction or conclusion. Band 3 3 There are some areas of conciseness. There may be occasional loss of focus or clarity. Own words (where appropriate) are used for most of the summary. Responses may be list-like or not well sequenced. Band 4 2 The summary is sometimes focused, but it may include comment, repetition, unnecessarily long explanation or lifted phrases. Band 5 1 The summary is unfocused or wordy. It may be answered in the wrong form (e.g. narrative, commentary or as notes). There may be frequent lifting of phrases and sentences. Band 6 0 Excessive lifting: no focus. The response cannot be understood or consists entirely of the words of the passage. (5)
